fact: January 20 is exactly one month away from the shortest day of the year in the Northern Hemisphere, which means daylight is finally getting noticeably longer!
In Antarctica, January 20 falls during the peak of summer when the sun never sets and penguins are busy feeding their fluffy gray chicks.
Tree sap in cold regions is at its thickest and stickiest around this time because the freezing temperatures turn it into nature's own maple syrup slush.
Why do your boogers freeze instantly when you breathe through your nose on January 20 in cold places? The air is so dry and cold that moisture turns solid in seconds!
Squirrels are at their fluffiest on dates like January 20 because their winter coats can be up to twice as thick as their summer fur.
The moon looks extra bright and crisp in late January because cold air holds less water vapor, making the sky clearer than a freshly cleaned window.
January 20 marks the time when many evergreen trees start secretly preparing for spring by moving nutrients from their roots up to their branches.
In the Northern Hemisphere, this date falls right in the middle of the season when your breath creates the biggest, puffiest clouds of vapor.
Owls are extra chatty around January 20 because it's peak mating season, and they're hooting love songs to each other all night long.
January 20 typically falls during Aquarius season, the zodiac sign represented by a water bearer.
Snow crystals formed on January 20 are more likely to be perfectly symmetrical because the air is cold enough and still enough for them to grow without getting bumped around.
